turns.content: I've been doing a lot of Java, so when I see something like this Rust code for building an AST it immediately reminds me of how we handle source spans in our parser. In Java we usually map tokens to lines and columns manually, but here the lexer seems to attach span info directly to each token before they even hit the grammar rules.

That's a great observation about how Rust differs from Java in this area. While Java often requires you to track line and column positions manually through a separate pass or annotation, Rust lets the lexer produce tokens that already carry their source spans as part of the token structure itself. This means your parser doesn't need an extra step to correlate tokens with their original location in the file.

Right, so when I parse this code into a tree it's not just about creating nodes but also preserving that span information for every node. Is there some way to make sure each AST node knows exactly where in the source file it came from?

Exactly, you're hitting on a key difference between how we handle spans and node creation here. In Rust we typically use an arena allocator for our AST nodes because that lets us allocate all the nodes in one go without worrying about memory fragmentation or leaking. This is much cleaner than Java where you might need to manage object references more explicitly.

I see, so the arena handles both the allocation and the lifetime of all these nodes together. I guess that's why we can have a single pointer to the root node and it implicitly knows everything about the whole tree structure.

That's right, and you can access any individual node through an index or by walking from the root. The arena also helps with memory safety since it tracks all allocations internally, which avoids some of those dangling pointer issues we sometimes see in Java when managing large object graphs.

Got it. So once I have this tree built I can walk through it using a visitor pattern to extract information like error locations or semantic analysis data without duplicating the traversal logic across different parts of my codebase.

Precisely, and that's why Rust has such nice support for this. The trait system lets you define a generic visitor interface that works on any AST node type, so you can write one visitor function instead of having separate implementations for each kind of expression or statement in your grammar.

That sounds really elegant. I wonder if there are any gotchas with the visitor pattern though? Like what happens if my AST contains some very unusual nested structures that might break standard traversal assumptions?

Good question, and you're right to be cautious about edge cases. Sometimes when your grammar allows for deeply nested or recursive constructs the visitor needs extra care to avoid stack overflows if using recursion instead of an iterative approach with a stack.
So I should probably use an explicit stack-based traversal here? Or is there something about how Rust's type system helps prevent those issues automatically?

You're right to think about that, and while Rust doesn't magically solve recursion problems, the way we structure our AST with proper bounds checking makes it easier to reason about. We can also use a library like `walk` or implement a simple iterative visitor using an explicit stack to avoid any deep recursion issues entirely.

Makes sense. I think the main takeaway is that Rust's combination of arena allocation and trait-based visitors really streamlines this compared to what we'd have to do in Java with manual memory management.

Absolutely, and that's why many teams are finding these patterns very useful when they're transitioning from high-level languages like Java to systems programming. The combination of safe memory allocation patterns and trait-based abstraction really makes building complex parsers much more manageable.
